notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary militia private; son-in-law of Jonathan Edwards

narrative_blocks.header: He marched in the Berkshire County militia
narrative_blocks.body: Elihu Parsons served as a private in the Berkshire County militia during the Revolution, under Captain Ezra Whittlesey and Colonel John Brown. He is recognized as a patriot ancestor by the Daughters of the American Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a daughter of Jonathan Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: In 1750 Parsons wed Sarah Edwards, a daughter of the theologian Jonathan Edwards, and the couple raised eleven children at Stockbridge. He lived as an esquire in the western Massachusetts hills.

sources.label: Salt of the Earth — David Brainerd and Jerusha Edwards: A Colonial Love Story

cross_connections.notes: The coincidence of two daughters marrying two Ingersolls in the same period and region (Berkshire County MA) likely reflects the prominence of the Ingersoll family in that area. (DAR records; FindAGrave)
cross_connections.display_label: Daughter Lydia married Aaron Ingersoll of Berkshire

cross_connections.notes: The coincidence of two daughters marrying two Ingersolls in the same period and region (Berkshire County MA) likely reflects the prominence of the Ingersoll family in that area. (DAR records; FindAGrave)
cross_connections.display_label: Daughter Sarah married David Ingersoll of Berkshire

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Edwards Parsons (1728-1805), eldest daughter of Jonathan Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest Edwards child born Sabbath afternoon, 1728
narrative_blocks.body: She was born August 25, 1728 in Northampton — 'on a Sabbath day, between 2 and 3 o'clock in the afternoon,' as her father Jonathan Edwards recorded. She was the eldest of his eleven children.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her father's attorney — Stockbridge farmer
narrative_blocks.body: In 1750 she married Elihu Parsons, a Northampton attorney who handled many of Jonathan Edwards's legal and personal affairs. Parsons later farmed in Stockbridge, where the family settled during the Mohican mission years.

narrative_blocks.header: Father warned suitor of her temper
narrative_blocks.body: When Elihu Parsons asked Jonathan Edwards for her hand, Edwards reportedly disclosed her difficult temper before quipping: 'I hope she has grace, but grace can live where you cannot.'

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters married into the Berkshire Ingersolls
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her eleven children — both daughters — married into the Ingersoll family of Berkshire County, Massachusetts. This is a separate Ingersoll line from the Loyalist New Haven Ingersolls who descended through the Newton-Miles line.

narrative_blocks.header: Cast the first iron ploughs in Canada
narrative_blocks.detail: Son of Eliphalet Parsons (H00904) and Martha Young; born 1789; baptized 1791 at Stockbridge MA. Moved through Broome County NY and Wilkes-Barre PA before relocating to Canada in 1824. Married Jennett Hepburn 1815. Two children: Lewis Eliphalet Parsons (HD1672, later provisional Governor of Alabama) and Fanny Jennette Parsons (HD1673, married Underground Railroad operator Seth Gates). Died September 2, 1827 at Chippewa (Chippawa), Ontario age 38. (Hinsdale family history; FindAGrave; WikiTree)
narrative_blocks.body: Erastus Parsons moved from Broome County NY to Upper Canada in 1824 and cast the first iron ploughs made in Canada before dying at 38.

sources.short: Avery, Genealogy of the Ingersoll Family in America (1926)
Descendants of William Smith of Glastonbury CT (archive.org)

notable.notable_blurb: Genealogy of the Ingersoll Family in America 1926

narrative_blocks.header: Thirteen children all reached adulthood and married
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Parsons married Deacon David Ingersoll II at Stockbridge MA on December 13, 1781. Their thirteen children all grew to adulthood, married, and mostly had large families — over 2,000 descendants are recorded. The Ingersoll-Yale lineage was one of the founding strata of Lee, Massachusetts; the marble from David's farm quarry helped build the U.S. Capitol.

narrative_blocks.header: Two thousand descendants and counting
narrative_blocks.body: The thirteen children she raised at Lee all married and “mostly had large families” — over two thousand descendants recorded by the 1926 Ingersoll genealogy, among them the founders of the Ingersoll Dollar Watch. Her widowed mother, a daughter of Jonathan Edwards, lived out years under her roof.

narrative_blocks.header: He cut down his own orchard
narrative_blocks.body: Finding that too many neighbors were getting drunk on his cider, Deacon Ingersoll felled every apple tree on his farm except those that fed his own table. At seventy-five he traveled to Ohio and Michigan to visit his children — before the railroads, “in the days of rugged constitutions.”

narrative_blocks.header: His quarry is in the Capitol
narrative_blocks.body: The marble quarry on his Lee farm, a mile and a half from the Congregational church he helped found, supplied “much of the marble in the Capitol in Washington.” The Ingersoll and Yale families, the genealogy says, “might well be called the founders of Lee.”

naming_inspiration.notes: Named for maternal grandfather Elihu Parsons Sr. (X00536), DAR patriot.

notable.notable_blurb: music professor at any American college (Oberlin 1834)

narrative_blocks.header: First music professor at any American college
narrative_blocks.body: In 1834, while still a Yale Divinity student, he was appointed Professor of Sacred Music and Principal of the Preparatory Department at Oberlin College. This was the first appointment of a professor of music at any American university.

narrative_blocks.header: Quit Rhode Island pulpit for Oberlin in 1835
narrative_blocks.body: He was ordained pastor of the Congregational Church in Woonsocket Rhode Island in December 1834. Eleven months later he resigned to take up the Oberlin music professorship, where he served almost five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Went to Kansas Territory for the anti-slavery cause
narrative_blocks.body: In April 1857 he resigned his Bloomington Illinois pulpit to go to Kansas in aid of the anti-slavery cause in the Kansas Territory. He labored at his own expense in the ministry near Burlingame until 1861.

narrative_blocks.header: First wife dead after sixteen months
narrative_blocks.body: He married Louisa Perry of Stockbridge in April 1835; she died sixteen months later in August 1836, leaving an infant son. He remarried Catharine Gillett in 1838 and had eight children with her.

narrative_blocks.header: Invented the first practical lawn mower (1847)
narrative_blocks.detail: In 1847, William Ketchum patented the first practical lawn mower in the United States. In 1861, he patented the Ketchum Hand Grenade — a cast iron oval head charged with gunpowder, an 8-inch wooden tail rod with four pasteboard fins, and a percussion cap nose. Made in 1, 3, and 5 lb sizes, approximately 93,200 were procured by the Union Army. The grenade was used at the Siege of Vicksburg and the Siege of Petersburg. A famous incident at Port Hudson: Confederate defenders learned to catch them in blankets and hurl them back — the grenades often exploded on return with greater force. The 1 and 3 lb sizes were preferred as they could be thrown farther.
narrative_blocks.body: Two inventions from the same man: the first practical U.S. lawn mower patent (1847) and the Ketchum Hand Grenade (U.S. Pat. #33,089, 1861), used across multiple Civil War sieges. About 93,200 were procured by the Union Army.

narrative_blocks.header: 14th Mayor of Buffalo, Millard Fillmore appointee
narrative_blocks.detail: William Ketchum served as Buffalo Village Trustee before the city's incorporation, then on the Erie County Board of Supervisors. He won election as the 14th Mayor of Buffalo on March 5, 1844 as the Whig candidate, serving until 1845 without seeking re-election. In 1851, President Millard Fillmore appointed him Collector of the Port of Buffalo. He co-founded the original Bank of Buffalo. He was a 6th-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker and a great-grandson of Rev. Jonathan Edwards through his mother Lucy Parsons.
narrative_blocks.body: Elected 14th Mayor of Buffalo as Whig candidate March 5, 1844. Appointed Collector of the Port of Buffalo by President Millard Fillmore in 1851.

narrative_blocks.header: Author: history of Buffalo and biography of Mohawk
narrative_blocks.detail: After retiring from the fur and hat business in 1857, William Ketchum devoted himself to history. In 1864 he published An Authentic and Comprehensive History of Buffalo (2 vols.), covering the city's early inhabitants including the Iroquois Six Nations and a sketch of Sir William Johnson. In 1872 he published a Memoir of Chief Joseph Brant (Thayendanegea), the celebrated Mohawk sachem and warrior. The files of the Buffalonian newspaper are archived at the Buffalo and Erie County Historical Society.
narrative_blocks.body: Wrote a 2-volume history of early Buffalo (1864) and a memoir of Mohawk chief Captain Joseph Brant (1872).

narrative_blocks.header: Colonial-era birth, Civil War year death, age ninety-four
narrative_blocks.body: Jerusha Parsons was born June 1, 1766 in Stockbridge Massachusetts, when the colonies still answered to George III. She died January 8, 1861 in Victor, Ontario County New York at age ninety-four — three months before Fort Sumter opened the Civil War. Her ninety-four-year life span covered the Revolution, the early Republic, Jacksonian democracy, abolitionism, and the war's first months.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband named in Bellamy's Shays' Rebellion novel
narrative_blocks.body: Jerusha's husband Ira Seymour Sr. served as deputy sheriff during Shays' Rebellion (1786-1787), siding with the 'aristocrats' Judge Timothy Edwards and Judge Jahleel Woodbridge against the Shays' insurgents — and as deputy sheriff he 'liberated many debtors from the county jail.' Edward Bellamy named him by name in the 1879 historical novel 'The Duke of Stockbridge,' the most-read fictional treatment of the rebellion. Jerusha's marriage thus placed her at the center of the Stockbridge gentry's response to the agrarian revolt.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband descended from Hartford co-founder Richard Seymour
narrative_blocks.body: Ira Seymour Sr. was the great-great-grandson of Richard Seymour (1596 England - 1655 Norwalk Connecticut) — one of the original founders of Hartford with Thomas Hooker in 1636. Jerusha's marriage thus united two completely independent Hartford-founding family lines through a Berkshire County Massachusetts match, two centuries after the original 1636 settlement. The Seymour-Parsons union is one of the cleaner cases in the tree of Hartford-founder lines reconverging.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named for Sarah Pierpont Edwards — maternal great-grandmother through Sarah Edwards (H00376) and her mother Sarah Pierpont (HD0418).
Named for Rev. Jonathan Edwards — maternal grandfather through Sarah Edwards Parsons (H00376).
